title: Kernel Architecture & Syscalls
description: The Linux kernel is the core of the operating system — it manages processes, memory, hardware, and provides the syscall interface that user-space programs use to interact with the system. At Enterprise, kernel knowledge is essential for debugging production incidents, building high-performance systems, and understanding container isolation.

sections.title: Kernel Architecture: User Space vs Kernel Space
sections.description: The kernel runs in a privileged mode (ring 0 on x86). User-space programs run in an unprivileged mode (ring 3). System calls (syscalls) are the only way user-space can request kernel services. The /proc and /sys filesystems expose kernel data structures to user space. At Enterprise, engineers working on infrastructure must understand this boundary — performance issues, security vulnerabilities, and debugging often hinge on crossing the kernel-user boundary.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Check kernel version ===
uname -a                            # Full kernel version and system info
uname -r                            # Just kernel release (e.g., 6.8.0-1015-aws)
cat /proc/version                   # Kernel version with gcc info

# === List loaded kernel modules ===
lsmod                               # All loaded modules
modinfo <module>                    # Module details (author, license, params)

# === Load/unload modules ===
sudo modprobe <module>              # Load module with dependency resolution
sudo insmod /path/to/module.ko      # Load module directly (no deps)
sudo rmmod <module>                 # Unload module
sudo modprobe -r <module>           # Unload with dependency cleanup

# === Kernel parameters (sysctl) ===
sysctl -a                           # List all kernel parameters
sysctl net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse         # Single parameter
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1  # Set parameter (runtime, non-persistent)
cat /etc/sysctl.conf                 # Persistent kernel parameters
sudo sysctl -p                       # Reload config from file

# === Key kernel subsystems visible via /proc ===
cat /proc/cpuinfo                   # CPU details (model, cache, flags)
cat /proc/meminfo                   # Memory details (Total, Free, Buffers, Cached)
cat /proc/zoneinfo                  # Memory zone info (NUMA)
cat /proc/interrupts                # IRQ distribution across CPUs
cat /proc/softirqs                  # Softirq stats
cat /proc/sched_debug               # Scheduler debug info

# === cgroups v2 (kernel resource control) ===
ls /sys/fs/cgroup/                  # Cgroup controllers
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu.stat         # CPU accounting
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.current   # Current memory usage

# === /sys — kernel objects ===
ls /sys/class/block/                # Block devices
ls /sys/class/net/                  # Network interfaces
cat /sys/class/net/eth0/device/vendor  # NIC vendor ID
cat /sys/block/sda/queue/scheduler   # I/O scheduler (mq-deadline, none, kyber)

# === Kernel ring buffer ===
dmesg                               # Kernel log messages
dmesg -w                            # Follow kernel log (like tail -f)
dmesg | grep -i error               # Kernel errors
dmesg -T                            # Human-readable timestamps

sections.title: System Calls Deep Dive
sections.description: Syscalls are the API between user-space and the kernel. Common syscalls: read, write, open, close, mmap, fork, execve, ioctl, epoll. Understanding syscalls is critical for debugging performance issues and understanding what your program is actually doing. At Enterprise, strace is one of the most valuable debugging tools for understanding 'why is this process slow?'
sections.codeBlock:
# === strace — trace system calls ===
strace ls                           # Trace all syscalls for 'ls'
strace -e openat,read ls            # Filter specific syscalls
strace -p 1234                      # Attach to running process
strace -c ls                        # Count syscalls (summary, great for profiling)
strace -f -c nginx                  # Include child processes
strace -tt -T ls                    # Show timestamps + syscall duration

# === Key syscalls to know ===
# Process:
#   fork()    — Create child process
#   execve()  — Replace process image
#   exit()    — Terminate process
#   wait()    — Wait for child to exit
ey
#   clone()   — Create thread (or process with CLONE_VM)
#
# File I/O:
#   open() / openat() — Open a file
#   read()            — Read from fd
#   write()           — Write to fd
#   close()           — Close fd
#   lseek()           — Reposition file offset
#   mmap()            — Memory-map a file
#
# Networking:
#   socket()   — Create endpoint
#   bind()     — Bind to address
#   listen()   — Listen for connections
#   accept()   — Accept connection
#   connect()  — Connect to remote
#   send()/recv() — Send/receive data
#   epoll_create() / epoll_wait() — Event-driven I/O

# === Syscall count per process ===
strace -c -p $(pgrep -f nginx | head -1) 2>&1 | head -20

# === Check syscall availability (seccomp) ===
# List syscalls allowed for a process
cat /proc/$(pgrep -f nginx | head -1)/status | grep -i seccomp
# 0 = disabled, 1 = strict, 2 = filter (seccomp-bpf)

# === /proc/sys/kernel/ — tune syscall behavior ===
cat /proc/sys/kernel/pid_max        # Max PID (default 32768, increase for large servers)
cat /proc/sys/kernel/threads-max    # Max threads system-wide
cat /proc/sys/fs/file-max           # Max open files system-wide

# === Syscall table ===
# Architecture-specific (x86_64 vs arm64 vs aarch64)
# List: /usr/include/x86_64-linux-gnu/asm/unistd_64.h
# Online: https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/arch/x86/entry/syscalls/syscall_64.tbl

sections.title: The Kernel Scheduler & Context Switching
sections.description: The Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) allocates CPU time to threads. Each thread gets a proportion of CPU based on its nice value and cgroup cpu.shares. The scheduler runs every context switch — when one thread is de-scheduled and another is scheduled. Context switches are not free — each one has overhead (TLB flush, cache miss). At Enterprise, high context-switch rates are a common performance issue in over-threaded applications.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Scheduler statistics ===
cat /proc/schedstat                 # Raw scheduler stats
cat /proc/sched_debug               # Detailed scheduler info

# === Context switches per process ===
cat /proc/<PID>/status | grep -i "ctxt_switches"
# voluntary_ctxt_switches — process gave up CPU (I/O wait, sleep)
# nonvoluntary_ctxt_switches — kernel preempted process (time slice expired)

# === System-wide context switches ===
vmstat 1 5                          # cs column = context switches/sec
# High context switches (>100k/sec per core) often indicate threading issues

# === Check scheduler policy ===
chrt -p <PID>                       # Show scheduling policy + priority
# SCHED_OTHER (0) — Default fair scheduling
# SCHED_FIFO (1)  — Real-time, first-in-first-out
# SCHED_RR (2)    — Real-time, round-robin
# SCHED_BATCH (3) — Batch scheduling (non-interactive)
# SCHED_IDLE (5)  — Lowest priority (idle-like)

# === CPU affinity ===
taskset -p <PID>                    # Show CPU affinity mask
taskset -cp <PID>                   # Show CPU affinity (list)
sudo taskset -cp 0-3 <PID>          # Pin to CPUs 0-3

# === NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) ===
nnumactl --hardware                  # NUMA topology
numastat -p <PID>                   # Memory allocation per NUMA node
# On multi-socket servers: accessing memory on remote socket is slower
# Enterprise pattern: pin processes + memory to same NUMA node for performance

# === IRQ affinity ===
# Distribute hardware interrupts across CPUs
cat /proc/irq/<IRQ>/smp_affinity     # CPU mask for this IRQ
# Set: echo 3 > /proc/irq/32/smp_affinity (send to CPUs 0+1)
# Or use irqbalance daemon for automatic distribution

# === /proc/loadavg ===
cat /proc/loadavg
# Load average = number of processes in running or uninterruptible state
# Over 1-minute, 5-minute, 15-minute windows
# Rule of thumb: load < #cores is normal; load > 2x#cores indicates overload

sections.title: Kernel Modules & Device Drivers
sections.description: Kernel modules are pieces of code that can be loaded/unloaded without rebooting. Most hardware drivers, filesystems, and network protocols are modules. At Enterprise, custom kernel modules are rare (most use the standard distribution kernel), but understanding the module system is essential for infosec (kernel rootkits load as modules) and for debugging hardware issues.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Module management ===
lsmod                               # All loaded modules
# Format: Module, Size, Used by

# Module details
modinfo nvme                        # Show info (author, description, params, licenses)
modinfo -p nvme                     # Just parameters

# Module dependency map
depmod                              # Build modules.dep
cat /lib/modules/$(uname -r)/modules.dep  # Module dependencies

# === Blacklist a module ===
echo "blacklist nouveau" | sudo tee /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-nvidia.conf

# === Module parameters ===
cat /sys/module/nvme/parameters/    # Runtime module parameters

echo "options nvme_core io_timeout=100" | sudo tee /etc/modprobe.d/nvme.conf

# === Check for kernel oops/crash ===
dmesg | grep -i "oops\|panic\|BUG"
ls /var/crash/                       # Crash dumps (kernel-core)

# === Kernel TLS / kTLS ===
lsmod | grep tls                     # Check if kTLS module loaded
# kTLS offloads TLS encryption to kernel (performance optimization)

# === Build a simple kernel module ===
# /usr/src/linux-headers-$(uname -r)/ must be installed
cat > hello.c << 'EOF'
#include <linux/module.h>
#include <linux/kernel.h>
#include <linux/init.h>

static int __init hello_init(void) {
    printk(KERN_INFO "Hello, Enterprise!\n");
    return 0;
}

static void __exit hello_exit(void) {
    printk(KERN_INFO "Goodbye, Enterprise!\n");
}

module_init(hello_init);
module_exit(hello_exit);
MODULE_LICENSE("GPL");
MODULE_AUTHOR("Production engineers");
EOF

# Makefile:
obj-m += hello.o
all:
	make -C /lib/modules/$(shell uname -r)/build M=$(PWD) modules
clean:
	make -C /lib/modules/$(shell uname -r)/build M=$(PWD) clean

# Build & test
make
sudo insmod hello.ko
dmesg | tail                        # See 'Hello, Enterprise!'
sudo rmmod hello
dmesg | tail                        # See 'Goodbye, Enterprise!'

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'What happens when you type a command like 'ls' in a shell?'

A: '(1) Shell reads the line and parses it. (2) Shell searches PATH for 'ls' binary (via execve syscall). (3) Shell calls fork() to create a child process. (4) Child calls execve('/usr/bin/ls', ['ls'], envp) — this replaces the child's process image with the ls binary. (5) The kernel loads the ELF binary, maps memory segments, sets up the stack. (6) ls runs, calls openat()/getdents64() to read directory entries. (7) ls calls write() to print results. (8) ls calls exit(0). (9) Shell's wait() returns with exit status. (10) Shell prints the next prompt.'

Q: 'What is a kernel panic and how do you debug it?'

A: 'A kernel panic is when the kernel encounters a fatal error it cannot recover from (e.g., null pointer dereference in kernel code, hardware error, or corrupted kernel data structure). The kernel prints a message to the console and either hangs or reboots. Debug via: (1) Check dmesg from the next boot — look for 'Oops' or 'panic' messages. (2) Enable kdump/kexec to capture a crash dump for analysis with crash utility. (3) Check /var/crash/ for vmcore files. (4) Use 'crash' utility to analyze: crash /usr/lib/debug/boot/vmlinux-$(uname -r) /var/crash/vmcore. At Enterprise, kernel panics are rare but critical — the response involves the Linux kernel team and hardware vendors.'

Q: 'What is the difference between a process and a thread in the kernel?'

A: 'In the Linux kernel, both processes and threads are implemented as 'tasks' (struct task_struct). The difference: threads share the same address space (mm_struct), file descriptors (files_struct), and signal handlers via the clone() syscall with CLONE_VM | CLONE_FILES | CLONE_SIGHAND flags. Processes have independent address spaces created via fork() or clone() without CLONE_VM. To the scheduler, they're all tasks — the kernel doesn't distinguish between processes and threads. This is why 'lightweight processes' (LWPs) is an accurate description of Linux threads.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Linux Kernel Documentation — Official kernel docs (admin-guide, core-api, process)
  • Linux Kernel Development — Robert Love, the best practical kernel guide
  • Linux Syscall Table — Interactive x86_64 syscall reference

learningObjectives: Understand kernel Architecture: User Space vs Kernel Space
Understand the Kernel Scheduler & Context Switching

analogy.title: Kernel as the OS Brain
analogy.description: The kernel is the central nervous system. System calls are nerve signals from body (userspace) to brain. Interrupts are reflexes - hardware taps. Scheduler decides which thought gets CPU time. Memory manager allocates brain space. Modules are loadable skills you can learn without rebooting the brain.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Kernel Architecture: User Space vs Kernel Space
practiceLabs.task: Run `uname` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man uname` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: System Calls Deep Dive
practiceLabs.task: Run `cat` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man cat` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: The Kernel Scheduler & Context Switching
practiceLabs.task: Run `lsmod` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man lsmod` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `uname` | Kernel Architecture: User Space vs Kernel Space |
| `cat` | Kernel Architecture: User Space vs Kernel Space |
| `lsmod` | Kernel Architecture: User Space vs Kernel Space |
| `strace` | System Calls Deep Dive |
| `ey` | System Calls Deep Dive |
| `cat` | System Calls Deep Dive |
| `cat` | The Kernel Scheduler & Context Switching |
| `vmstat` | The Kernel Scheduler & Context Switching |
| `chrt` | The Kernel Scheduler & Context Switching |
| `lsmod` | Kernel Modules & Device Drivers |
| `modinfo` | Kernel Modules & Device Drivers |
| `depmod` | Kernel Modules & Device Drivers |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man uname`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: kernel operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Kernel Architecture: User Space vs Kernel Space?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Kernel Architecture: User Space vs Kernel Space is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `uname` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the kernel directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a kernel directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a kernel scenario, your monitoring alerts show uname failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  You don't need to be a kernel developer to work at FAANG, but you need to understand what the kernel does
FAANG, kernel upgrades are rolled out in canary phases: 1% of hosts, then 10%, then 50%, then full rollout, with automated rollback on any metric regression
productionTips: Production Tip:  Before deploying a new kernel version at scale, always test on a non-production instance first
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, kernel architecture & syscalls is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
